suggestions.name: StringComparator
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive comparison unit that implements various string comparison strategies with null-safe handling. Extracting them into a StringComparator class creates a focused, reusable component that follows the Comparator pattern and separates comparison concerns from general string utilities.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: comparing two strings and returning an integer result following the standard Java comparator contract (-1, 0, 1). They handle null-safe string comparison with options for case sensitivity and null ordering behavior.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string comparison" domain. They implement variations of the Comparator pattern for strings, providing consistent comparison logic with different behavioral options (case-sensitive vs case-insensitive, null handling preferences).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Improves Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates comparison logic from other string utilities
- Enhances Reusability: Can be used as a standalone comparator or injected into sorting algorithms
- Reduces Complexity: Removes comparison concerns from the large StringUtils class
- Better Testability: Comparison logic can be tested in isolation with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
Follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to create a specialized comparator. This aligns with the Strategy pattern, where different comparison behaviors are encapsulated in a cohesive unit. The extracted class will have high cohesion (all methods perform string comparison) and low coupling (minimal dependencies).

suggestions.name: StringSplitter
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for splitting strings into arrays based on various separator criteria. They share common splitting algorithms, work with the same data types (String input, String[] output), and provide complementary functionality for different splitting scenarios. Extracting them creates a focused, reusable component dedicated to string tokenization operations.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a single, well-defined responsibility: splitting strings into arrays based on various separator criteria. All public methods are overloaded variants of string splitting functionality, with options for different separator types (char, string, whitespace), maximum split limits, and token preservation behavior. The private splitWorker methods contain the core splitting algorithms optimized for different scenarios.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
All methods belong to the "string tokenization" domain. They represent a cohesive subsystem for parsing strings into constituent parts based on delimiter patterns. The methods work together to provide a comprehensive string splitting API with performance optimizations for common cases (single character separators, whitespace splitting).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string splitting logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: String splitting functionality can be reused across different contexts without importing the entire StringUtils class
- Complexity Reduction: Removes a significant chunk of specialized logic from the god class, making it more focused
- Testability: String splitting behavior can be tested in isolation with dedicated test cases
- Performance: Allows for focused optimization of splitting algorithms without affecting other utilities

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate a cohesive set of related methods that perform string tokenization. The extraction creates a specialized component with high cohesion (all methods split strings) and low coupling (minimal dependencies on external utilities). This aligns with the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class dedicated solely to string splitting operations.

suggestions.name: StringAligner
suggestions.rationale: These methods form a cohesive unit responsible for string alignment and padding operations (left, right, center). They share common padding logic, constants, and work together to provide comprehensive text positioning capabilities. Extracting them creates a focused class that handles the single responsibility of string alignment and formatting.
suggestions.reasoning:
Step 1: Primary Responsibility Analysis
These methods share a primary responsibility of string padding and alignment operations. The core functionality includes left padding (leftPad), right padding (rightPad), centering (center), and some string manipulation utilities like chomp, removeEnd, and repeat. All methods focus on adjusting string length and positioning through padding characters or strings.

Step 2: Shared Concept/Domain
These methods belong to the "string formatting" domain, specifically text alignment and padding operations. They work together to provide comprehensive string positioning capabilities - whether you need to align text left, right, or center within a specified width. The methods share common padding logic, use the same constants (SPACE, PAD_LIMIT), and have similar parameter patterns (string, size, padding character/string).

Step 3: Extraction Benefits
- Single Responsibility Principle: Isolates string alignment/padding logic from other string utilities
- Code Reusability: Text alignment is commonly needed across different contexts (reports, console output, formatting)
- Complexity Reduction: Removes specialized formatting logic from the general StringUtils class
- Testability: Alignment logic can be tested independently with focused test cases

Step 4: Design Justification
This follows the Extract Class refactoring pattern to separate formatting concerns. The methods exhibit high cohesion (all related to text positioning/alignment) and can operate independently with minimal external dependencies. This creates a specialized formatter that adheres to the Single Responsibility Principle.
